update.headline: Artemis II crew safely returns to Earth after historic 10-day lunar mission.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026.
Crew members Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent health aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission set a new human distance record, reaching 252,756 miles from Earth, breaking the Apollo 13 record.
Orion's heat shield successfully withstood 5,000°F during re-entry with a peak velocity of 24,664 mph.
update.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
update.lastEvent: Successful splashdown in the Pacific Ocean and recovery by the USS John P. Murtha.
update.nextEvent: Transport of crew to Johnson Space Center and Orion capsule return to Naval Base San Diego.

milestones.label: Launch
milestones.detail: Successful launch of SLS and Orion from KSC Pad 39B.

milestones.label: Core Stage Separation
milestones.detail: MECO & core stage jettison

milestones.label: Solar Array Deploy
milestones.detail: Four Orion solar wings extended

milestones.label: Perigee Raise
milestones.detail: Orion performed perigee raise and apogee raise burns.

milestones.label: Proximity Ops
milestones.detail: Crew successfully demonstrated manual piloting of Orion near the ICPS upper stage.

milestones.label: Translunar Injection
milestones.detail: Translunar injection will occur approximately two hours after launch once flight is underway.

milestones.label: ICPS Separation
milestones.detail: Upper stage jettisoned

milestones.label: AVATAR Scientific Payload Checkout
milestones.detail: Testing 'organ-on-a-chip' technology to study deep space radiation effects on bone marrow cells.

milestones.label: Earth Photography By Crew
milestones.detail: Crew captured images of Earth's auroras and zodiacal light.

milestones.label: Outbound Trajectory Correction (OTC-1)
milestones.detail: Trajectory correction burn was scrapped due to high precision of the TLI burn.

milestones.label: Hygiene Bay Inspection
milestones.detail: Non-critical smell from waste management system identified as a dormant heater unit.

milestones.label: Lunar Distance Equalization
milestones.detail: Orion officially became closer to the Moon than to Earth on Flight Day 4.

milestones.label: Thermal Protection Inspection
milestones.detail: External inspection confirmed heat shield tiles are in excellent condition.

milestones.label: Manual Pilot Demo
milestones.detail: Koch and Hansen tested Orion handling qualities in deep space for 41 minutes.

milestones.label: Urine System Sun-Soak
milestones.detail: Spacecraft maneuvered to warm the venting system and resolve a temperature-related blockage.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Orion officially surpassed the record set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970.

milestones.label: Otc 3
milestones.detail: Refined Orion’s path for the lunar flyby with a 17.5-second burn.

milestones.label: Deep Space Cpr Demo
milestones.detail: Astronatus practiced emergency medical procedures in microgravity.

milestones.label: Archer Study Participation
milestones.detail: Crew monitoring physiological responses to deep-space radiation via wearable sensors.

milestones.label: Waste System Resolution
milestones.detail: Ground teams and crew successfully resolved intermittent issues with the onboard toilet system.

milestones.label: Urine Line Freeze
milestones.detail: Issue identified as a blocked filter rather than ice; crew utilizing backup systems.

milestones.label: Laser Comm Milestone
milestones.detail: Downlinked over 100GB of high-resolution data via laser-based communications system.

milestones.label: CSA Live Downlink
milestones.detail: Crew engaged in a live media event with students from across Canada.

milestones.label: Suit Systems Test
milestones.detail: Crew verified fit, pressurization, and mobility of OCSS suits in microgravity.

milestones.label: Orientale Basin Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured the first live close-up images of the Moon's western limb Orientale Basin since the Apollo era.

milestones.label: Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Orion officially entered the Lunar Sphere of Influence where lunar gravity becomes the dominant force.

milestones.label: Female Distance Record
milestones.detail: Christina Koch officially sets the record for the farthest distance from Earth for a woman.

milestones.label: Lunar Crater Naming Ceremony
milestones.detail: Crew unofficially named a lunar crater 'Carroll' in honor of Commander Wiseman's late wife.

milestones.label: Lunar Surface Observation
milestones.detail: Began 7-hour observation period of 35 geological lunar features.

milestones.label: Communications Blackout
milestones.detail: 40-minute loss of signal during the far-side lunar transit.

milestones.label: Lunar Flyby
milestones.detail: Orion reached closest approach of 6,545 km above the lunar surface.

milestones.label: Human Distance Record
milestones.detail: Reached record distance of 252,756 miles (406,771 km) from Earth.

milestones.label: Return Coast
milestones.detail: Spacecraft transitioned to an Earthbound free-return trajectory following lunar flyby.

milestones.label: Deep Space Solar Eclipse
milestones.detail: Observed 54 minutes of solar corona totality during a unique deep-space eclipse.

milestones.label: Farthest Distance From Earth Record
milestones.detail: New record for farthest human-rated spacecraft distance: 252,756 miles (406,778 km).

milestones.label: Earthrise Photo Ops
milestones.detail: Crew captured high-definition Earthset imagery as Earth disappeared behind the lunar limb.

milestones.label: Glushko Crater Imaging
milestones.detail: Captured high-resolution imagery of the Glushko Crater from lunar proximity.

milestones.label: Sans Medical Exams
milestones.detail: The crew is performing intraocular pressure monitoring to study spaceflight-associated vision changes.

milestones.label: Broke Apollo 13 Distance Record
milestones.detail: Mission reached a record-breaking distance of 406,771 km from Earth.

milestones.label: Impact Flash Observations
milestones.detail: The crew documented four meteoroid impact flashes on the lunar surface.

milestones.label: AVATAR Research Project
milestones.detail: Ongoing study of radiation and microgravity effects on organ-on-a-chip tissue analogs.

milestones.label: Interactive Public Engagement
milestones.detail: Crew discussed the 'overview effect' and shared high-resolution lunar far side imagery.

milestones.label: Exit Lunar Sphere Of Influence
milestones.detail: Spacecraft successfully transitioned back into Earth's sphere of influence.

milestones.label: Return Trajectory Correction 1
milestones.detail: Completed a 15-second thruster ignition to adjust velocity by 1.6 feet per second.

milestones.label: ISS Crew Conversation
milestones.detail: Conducted the first-ever radio linkup between a lunar mission and the ISS.

milestones.label: Radiation Shelter Demo
milestones.detail: Crew practiced emergency radiation shelter construction using onboard equipment.

milestones.label: Manual Piloting Demonstration #2
milestones.detail: Canceled due to technical monitoring of LOX pressurization system.

milestones.label: Orthostatic Garment Test
milestones.detail: Crew testing compression suits to maintain circulation during transition to 1G.

milestones.label: Day 9 Comm Blackout
milestones.detail: A brief return link loss caused a temporary communication blackout that was quickly resolved.

milestones.label: Reentry Configuration
milestones.detail: Crew configured the cabin and installed seats for high-G descent.

milestones.label: Crew News Conference
milestones.detail: Live downlink session for the crew to answer reporter questions.

milestones.label: Manual Pilot Test Final
milestones.detail: Final evaluation of Orion’s handling characteristics in proximity to Earth.

milestones.label: Rtc 2 Burn
milestones.detail: Second return trajectory correction burn successfully completed.

milestones.label: AVATAR Investigation Conclusion
milestones.detail: Real-time monitoring of radiation effects via Organ-on-a-Chip technology concluded.

milestones.label: Final Cabin Stowage
milestones.detail: Crew completed science stowage and cabin cleanup for return.

milestones.label: Rtc 3
milestones.detail: Final trajectory adjustment burn completed to align for the Pacific splashdown target.

milestones.label: Service Module Jettison
milestones.detail: The service module was jettisoned to expose the heat shield for atmospheric entry.

milestones.label: Re-Entry
milestones.detail: Orion entered the atmosphere at 24,664 mph, surviving temperatures of 5,000°F.

milestones.label: Drogue Parachute Deployment
milestones.detail: Two drogue parachutes will deploy at 22,000 feet to stabilize the capsule.

milestones.label: Splashdown
milestones.detail: Precision splashdown within 0.6 miles of target recovery zone.

milestones.label: Crew Recovery
milestones.detail: Crew safely transferred to the USS John P. Murtha for medical evaluation.

milestones.label: Post-Splashdown News Conference
milestones.detail: Engineering debrief covering heat shield performance and propulsion systems.

milestones.label: Houston Crew Arrival
milestones.detail: Crew is expected to be flown back to Johnson Space Center for family reunions.

milestones.label: Arrival At Naval Base San Diego
milestones.detail: The recovery ship is expected to dock in San Diego within the next 24-48 hours.

history.headline: Artemis II crew safely returns to Earth after historic 10-day lunar mission.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026.
Crew members Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent health aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission set a new human distance record, reaching 252,756 miles from Earth, breaking the Apollo 13 record.
Orion's heat shield successfully withstood 5,000°F during re-entry with a peak velocity of 24,664 mph.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown in the Pacific Ocean and recovery by the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Transport of crew to Johnson Space Center and Orion capsule return to Naval Base San Diego.

history.headline: Artemis II mission successfully concludes with Orion splashdown and safe crew recovery.
details: The Orion spacecraft Integrity splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 5:07 p.m. PDT on April 10, marking the first human lunar return mission in over 50 years.
Crew members Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and walked unaided across the deck of the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission reached a record-breaking distance of 406,771 km from Earth and achieved a closest lunar approach of 6,545 km.
Orion's heat shield successfully endured re-entry temperatures of 5,000°F after hitting the atmosphere at Mach 32.
sources: U.S. Navy Recovery Force (USS John P. Murtha)
history.crewStatus: Crew is in excellent health and 'green' for travel to Houston following initial medical evaluation.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown and recovery of the Orion capsule and crew off the coast of San Diego.
history.nextEvent: Crew homecoming celebration at NASA Johnson Space Center in Houston.

history.headline: Artemis II successfully concludes with splashdown in the Pacific Ocean and crew recovery.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down at 00:07 UTC on April 11, ending a 9-day historic lunar journey.
The crew was recovered by the USS John P. Murtha and is reported to be in excellent health and spirits.
Orion is secured in the ship's well deck for transport to San Diego and eventually back to Kennedy Space Center.
Data from the 1.13 million km mission will be used to finalize systems for the Artemis III lunar landing mission.
history.crewStatus: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are walking unassisted and in good spirits aboard the recovery vessel.
history.lastEvent: Successful recovery of the Orion capsule and crew by the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Arrival at Naval Base San Diego and subsequent crew flight to Johnson Space Center in Houston.

history.headline: Artemis II Orion spacecraft splashes down safely, concluding a historic 10-day lunar journey.
details: The Orion capsule Integrity successfully splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07:27 UTC on April 11, 2026.
Crew members Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission lasted 9 days, 1 hour, 32 minutes, and 15 seconds, setting a new human distance record from Earth.
Orion will be transported to Kennedy Space Center for a detailed post-flight analysis of the heat shield and flight systems.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in high spirits and adjusting to Earth's gravity.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown and crew recovery by the USS John P. Murtha southwest of San Diego.
history.nextEvent: Crew arrival at Ellington Field in Houston for family reunions and medical monitoring.

history.headline: Artemis II mission successfully concludes with Orion splashdown and recovery in the Pacific Ocean.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down off the coast of San Diego at 8:07 p.m. EDT on April 10, 2026.
Recovery operations were led by the USS John P. Murtha, which successfully secured the capsule in its well deck.
Astronauts Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent health following initial medical evaluations.
The mission traveled 694,481 miles and set a new human spaceflight distance record of 406,771 km from Earth.
history.crewStatus: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and returning to Houston.
history.lastEvent: Initial medical checkout and extraction of the crew via helicopter to the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Crew arrival at Johnson Space Center and formal post-mission press conference.

history.headline: Artemis II crew safely returns to Earth after historic 10-day lunar mission.
details: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' successfully splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11.
The crew of four is in excellent health and currently resting aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission set a new human distance record from Earth, reaching 406,771 km (252,756 miles).
Heat shield successfully protected the capsule from re-entry temperatures of 2,760°C.
sources: U.S. Navy Recovery Task Force Reports
history.crewStatus: Crew is in excellent health; currently undergoing medical evaluations aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
history.lastEvent: Splashdown and crew recovery by the USS John P. Murtha recovery team.
history.nextEvent: Arrival of the crew at Ellington Field in Houston for a homecoming ceremony.

history.headline: Artemis II mission concludes successfully with historic Pacific Ocean splashdown.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down on target at 00:07 UTC on April 11, concluding a 9-day, 1-hour, and 32-minute mission.
Crew members Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and adapting to 1g aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission set a new record for farthest human travel from Earth, reaching a maximum distance of 406,771 km (252,756 miles).
NASA and Navy teams have secured the spacecraft and are preparing the crew for their return to Houston today.
history.crewStatus: The four-person crew is in excellent health and high spirits; they are currently aboard the recovery vessel awaiting transport to Houston.
history.lastEvent: Crew was successfully recovered by the USS John P. Murtha and completed initial medical evaluations.
history.nextEvent: Homecoming ceremony at Ellington Field in Houston, scheduled between 20:00 UTC and 01:00 UTC.

history.headline: Artemis II crew safely returns to Earth after historic 9-day lunar flyby mission.
details: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026.
The crew set a new human deep-space distance record, reaching 406,777 km from Earth.
Astronauts are currently undergoing medical evaluations aboard the USS John P. Murtha and are in good health.
Recovery teams have secured the capsule in the ship's well deck for transport to San Diego.
history.crewStatus: Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in good shape and spirits; they are undergoing standard post-landing health checks.
history.lastEvent: Successful extraction of the crew from the Orion capsule followed by recovery of the spacecraft.
history.nextEvent: Transport of the crew from the USS John P. Murtha to Johnson Space Center in Houston.

history.headline: Artemis II successfully concludes with Pacific Ocean splashdown, setting new human deep-space records.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026, after a 9-day lunar mission.
The crew reached a record-breaking distance of 406,771 km from Earth, surpassing the 1970 record held by Apollo 13.
Astronauts Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent health aboard the USS John P. Murtha recovery ship.
NASA confirms Artemis III remains on track for a 2028 target following the performance success of the Orion spacecraft.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and currently undergoing medical evaluations.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown and recovery of the Orion capsule and crew by the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Arrival of the crew at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston for family reunions.

history.headline: Artemis II successfully concludes as the Orion spacecraft splashes down in the Pacific Ocean.
details: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down at 00:07 UTC on April 11 off the coast of San Diego, ending a 10-day mission.
The crew was successfully recovered within an hour of splashdown and is reported to be in excellent health.
The mission achieved a historic human distance record of 252,756 miles from Earth.
Orion's heat shield successfully withstood atmospheric re-entry temperatures of 5,000°F at speeds of Mach 33.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown in the Pacific Ocean followed by safe crew recovery and medical evaluation.
history.nextEvent: Crew return to NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston to reunite with families.

history.headline: Artemis II successfully concludes with Pacific Ocean splashdown, returning humans to Earth from the Moon for the first time in 50 years.
details: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down 60 miles off San Diego at 00:07:27 UTC, ending the 10-day lunar mission.
Crew members Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health aboard the USS John P. Murtha.
The mission reached a peak distance of 406,771 km, breaking the record for the farthest humans have ever traveled from Earth.
The Orion capsule is being secured in the ship's well deck for transport to Naval Base San Diego and Kennedy Space Center.
sources: USS John P. Murtha Recovery Team
history.crewStatus: The crew is in excellent health and high spirits; they are currently aboard the USS John P. Murtha and will soon fly to Houston.
history.lastEvent: Successful parachute-assisted splashdown and crew recovery by the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Crew arrival at Johnson Space Center in Houston to reunite with families and begin re-adaptation.

history.currentPhase: Crew recovery and post-flight medical evaluations
history.headline: Artemis II mission concludes successfully with record-breaking splashdown in the Pacific Ocean.
details: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' completed a bullseye splashdown at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026, after a 9-day mission.
The mission set a new record for the farthest humans have traveled from Earth, reaching 406,778 km (252,760 miles).
All four crew members are in excellent health and were recovered by the USS John P. Murtha within two hours.
The heat shield successfully withstood re-entry temperatures of 5,000°F while traveling at 39,750 km/h.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, Specialist Koch, and Specialist Hansen are in excellent health and walked unaided onto the recovery ship.
history.lastEvent: Crew recovery and extraction to the USS John P. Murtha.
history.nextEvent: Crew transfer from the recovery ship to Houston and detailed post-flight debriefs.

history.headline: Artemis II Orion spacecraft splashes down in Pacific Ocean, successfully completing historic crewed lunar mission.
details: The crew of four astronauts was safely recovered by the USS John P. Murtha and are reported in excellent health.
Mission set a new human-rated spacecraft distance record of 406,778 km from Earth on Day 6.
Orion executed a hybrid re-entry, hitting the atmosphere at 40,000 kph and enduring temperatures of 2,760°C.
The spacecraft will be transported to San Diego before returning to Kennedy Space Center for post-flight analysis.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and undergoing standard evaluations in the medical bay of the USS John P. Murtha.
history.lastEvent: Successful splashdown and extraction of the crew by U.S. Navy helicopters.
history.nextEvent: Post-splashdown news conference at 02:35 UTC followed by crew transit to Johnson Space Center.

history.headline: Artemis II successfully concludes with historic Orion splashdown and safe recovery of four-person crew.
details: The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07 UTC on April 11, 2026.
Crew members Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are in excellent health aboard the recovery ship USS John P. Murtha.
The mission set a new human distance record, reaching 252,756 miles (406,771 km) from Earth.
Orion's heat shield successfully endured re-entry temperatures of approximately 5,000°F (2,760°C).
history.crewStatus: The crew is reported to be in excellent health and high spirits in the medical bay of the USS John P. Murtha.
history.lastEvent: Safe extraction of the four-person crew and transport to the recovery vessel.
history.nextEvent: Post-splashdown news conference and transport of Orion capsule to Naval Base San Diego.

history.headline: Artemis II mission concludes successfully with Pacific Ocean splashdown and crew recovery.
details: The Orion capsule Integrity splashed down in the Pacific Ocean at 00:07:27 UTC, approximately 50 miles off the San Diego coast.
The USS John P. Murtha recovery team successfully extracted all four crew members from the capsule approximately 90 minutes after landing.
The mission set a new record for farthest human travel from Earth at 252,756 miles, surpassing the Apollo 13 record.
Orion successfully executed a 'skip re-entry' maneuver, withstanding temperatures of 5,000°F before parachute deployment.
sources: USS John P. Murtha Recovery Team
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and undergoing standard post-flight medical evaluations.
history.lastEvent: Crew successfully extracted from Orion capsule and hoisted via helicopter to the recovery ship.
history.nextEvent: Post-splashdown technical news conference from NASA Johnson Space Center at 02:30 UTC.

history.currentPhase: Orion Crew Module has separated from the service module and is preparing for atmospheric reentry and splashdown.
history.headline: Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' separates from service module in preparation for high-speed Earth reentry.
details: Service module jettison occurred at 23:33 UTC, exposing the heat shield for entry interface.
The crew is strapped into their Orion Crew Survival System suits for a direct descent trajectory.
Entry interface at 400,000 feet is expected at 23:53 UTC, followed by a 6-minute plasma communications blackout.
Splashdown is targeted for 00:07 UTC on April 11 in the Pacific Ocean near San Diego.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, Specialist Koch, and Specialist Hansen are in excellent spirits and have completed final leak checks on their survival suits.
history.lastEvent: Successful separation of the Orion Crew Module from its service module at 23:33 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Entry interface at 400,000 feet followed by a 6-minute communications blackout.

history.currentPhase: Mission day 10: Final approach for atmospheric reentry and Pacific splashdown.
history.headline: Artemis II crew prepares for splashdown following successful final trajectory correction burn.
details: The crew completed the third and final return trajectory correction burn (RTC-3) to refine the reentry angle.
The Orion spacecraft 'Integrity' is rapidly accelerating toward Earth, currently within 61,326 miles at start of the crew day.
Recovery teams aboard the USS John P. Murtha are on station for the scheduled 8:07 p.m. ET splashdown.
The mission has successfully surpassed the Apollo 13 distance record, reaching 252,756 miles from Earth.
history.crewStatus: Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and are securing the cabin for reentry.
history.lastEvent: Completion of the third return trajectory correction burn at 18:53 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Service Module jettison scheduled for 23:33 UTC (7:33 p.m. ET).

history.headline: Artemis II crew prepares for Pacific splashdown following final return trajectory burn.
details: Orion completed its third and final trajectory correction burn at 18:53 UTC to align for atmospheric entry.
The crew has donned OCSS pressure suits and completed cabin stowage in preparation for module separation.
Spacecraft will hit the atmosphere at Mach 35, enduring heat shield temperatures of 5,000°F.
Recovery forces including the USS John P. Murtha are on station for the target splashdown near San Diego.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Final return trajectory correction burn (RTC-3) completed at 18:53 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Jettison of the Service Module scheduled for 23:33 UTC.

history.headline: Orion spacecraft Integrity enters final hours of flight ahead of Pacific Ocean splashdown.
details: Final trajectory correction burn (RTC-3) was successfully completed at 18:53 UTC to fine-tune entry path.
Orion is scheduled for atmospheric interface at 23:53 UTC at a speed of approximately 24,000 mph.
NASA and Navy recovery teams are in position off the San Diego coast for a 00:07 UTC splashdown.
Crew remains in excellent health after breaking the human deep-space distance record.
history.crewStatus: Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits.
history.lastEvent: Final trajectory correction burn (RTC-3) completed at 18:53 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Service Module jettison followed by atmospheric reentry and splashdown.

history.headline: Orion spacecraft Integrity enters final hours of mission ahead of Pacific splashdown.
details: The crew completed a final 8-second trajectory correction burn at 18:53 UTC to refine their Earth entry point.
Astronauts Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen are suiting up in OCSS survival suits and securing the cabin for high-G reentry.
The USS John P. Murtha recovery ship is currently on station near San Diego to retrieve the crew and capsule.
Orion is accelerating under Earth's gravity and will hit the atmosphere at approximately 24,000 mph.
history.crewStatus: Commander Wiseman, Pilot Glover, and Specialists Koch and Hansen are in excellent health and high spirits as they prepare for splashdown.
history.lastEvent: Final 8-second trajectory correction burn completed at 18:53 UTC.
history.nextEvent: Separation of the Orion Service Module at 23:25 UTC.
